past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed 10 years ago, managed with medication
Benign prostatic hyperplasia, diagnosed 3 years ago
Chronic constipation, worsening over the past 4 years
REM sleep behavior disorder, diagnosed 2 years ago by sleep medicine
Orthostatic lightheadedness, evaluated 1 year ago, attributed to antihypertensive medication
family_history: Father died at age 78 of myocardial infarction, no neurological history
Mother alive at age 84, has essential tremor diagnosed in her 60s
No known family history of Parkinson's disease or dementia

social_history.occupation: Civil engineer, recently transitioned to a supervisory desk role due to difficulty with field inspections
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife in a single-story ranch home. Two adult children live nearby
social_history.tobacco: Former smoker, quit 20 years ago, 10 pack-year history
social_history.exercise: Used to jog 3 times per week, stopped about 18 months ago due to balance concerns; now walks daily with his wife

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, date, and situation. MoCA 25/30 with points lost on trail-making (1 error), delayed recall (2/5 words, retrieved 1 additional with category cue), and verbal fluency (10 animals in 60 seconds). Speech is mildly hypophonic with reduced prosody. No aphasia. Attention and concentration intact on digit span forward (7) and backward (4).
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. No vertical gaze palsy. Facial expression is symmetric but mildly hypomimic bilaterally. Jaw jerk normal. Hearing grossly intact. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline, no fasciculations. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength intact.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk normal throughout. Tone: bilateral cogwheel rigidity at wrists, symmetric; mild axial rigidity on neck passive range of motion. Mild bilateral bradykinesia on finger tapping and hand opening-closing, symmetric, without clear side predominance. No appendicular or truncal tremor at rest or with action. No myoclonus. Strength 5/5 throughout all extremities. No pronator drift.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch and pinprick intact throughout. Proprioception intact at great toes bilaterally. Vibration mildly reduced at bilateral ankles. No cortical sensory deficits.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric in biceps, triceps, brachioradialis bilaterally. Patellar reflexes 2+ bilaterally. Achilles reflexes 1+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. No Hoffmann sign. No palmomental reflex.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose intact bilaterally with no dysmetria. Heel-to-shin mildly slow bilaterally but no frank cerebellar dysmetria. Rapid alternating movements mildly slow and decremented bilaterally, symmetric.
neurological_exam.gait: Mildly wide-based gait. Reduced arm swing bilaterally. Short stride length. Difficulty with tandem gait (3 missteps in 10 steps). Pull test: retropulsion with 2 steps backward to recover. No freezing. Turns are performed en bloc with 4-5 steps.
neurological_exam.additional: Orthostatic vital signs: supine BP 142/88 mmHg, HR 72; standing at 3 minutes BP 108/64 mmHg, HR 78 (systolic drop of 34 mmHg with inadequate heart rate compensation). Patient reports mild lightheadedness on standing. No inspiratory stridor. No stimulus-sensitive myoclonus. Applause sign: 7 claps (unable to stop at 3, suggestive of frontal dysfunction). No alien limb phenomenon. No limb apraxia.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive difficulty with balance and slowing of movements over the past 2 years
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Thomas Kelleher is a 60-year-old right-handed civil engineer who presents to the neurology movement disorders clinic for evaluation of progressive gait instability and generalized motor slowing that has gradually worsened over the past two years. His wife, who accompanies him, first noticed changes approximately two years ago when he began having difficulty maintaining his balance on uneven terrain during construction site inspections. He had two falls at work sites within a six-month period, one resulting in a minor forearm abrasion. Around the same time, his wife observed that his walking had become slower with shorter steps, and that he appeared stiffer overall. She notes that he no longer swings his arms while walking and that getting out of a chair has become effortful. Notably, his symptoms have appeared symmetric from the onset, with no clear lateralization. Neither the patient nor his wife has noticed any tremor. Over the past year, his handwriting has become smaller, and he has had increasing difficulty with fine motor tasks such as buttoning shirts and using a computer mouse, though again these difficulties are bilateral. His speech has become quieter and more monotone, which colleagues have commented upon. He has had significant autonomic symptoms predating his motor complaints. He developed erectile dysfunction approximately three years ago, which was attributed to his antihypertensive medication, though dose reduction did not improve symptoms. Chronic constipation has been worsening for four years, requiring daily polyethylene glycol. Most notably, he was diagnosed with REM sleep behavior disorder two years ago after his wife reported that he was acting out dreams, shouting, and flailing his arms during sleep. He once punched her inadvertently while asleep. He has also experienced orthostatic lightheadedness over the past year, particularly when rising from bed in the morning or standing up quickly from a seated position. He had one syncopal episode three months ago while standing in line at the grocery store. A cardiology evaluation including echocardiogram and Holter monitor was unremarkable. He denies visual hallucinations, urinary incontinence, dysphagia, or significant cognitive complaints, though his wife notes he has become somewhat apathetic and less engaged in hobbies he previously enjoyed, such as woodworking. There have been no episodes of confusion or fluctuating alertness.

signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: Subtle hyperintense rim along the posterolateral margin of bilateral putamina
signal_characteristics.T1: Mild volume reduction of bilateral putamina
signal_characteristics.SWI: Subtle hypointensity in the dorsolateral putamina bilaterally, possibly reflecting iron deposition

signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: No pontine cross sign (hot cross bun sign absent)

signal_characteristics.T1_sagittal: Midbrain area within normal limits; no hummingbird sign

signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: Punctate periventricular white matter hyperintensities, age-appropriate

signal_characteristics.T1: Ventricles are proportionate to sulcal prominence, no disproportionate ventriculomegaly

volumetrics.putaminal_volume: Mildly reduced bilaterally, symmetric, below 15th percentile for age

additional_observations: No acute infarcts on DWI sequences
No mass lesions or pathological enhancement post-gadolinium
No evidence of cerebral microbleeds on SWI
No basal ganglia lacunar infarcts to suggest vascular parkinsonism
No ventriculomegaly disproportionate to sulcal atrophy (no NPH pattern)
mri.impression: Subtle bilateral putaminal volume loss with possible increased iron deposition and a faint T2/FLAIR hyperintense rim along the posterolateral putamina bilaterally. No cerebellar or pontine atrophy. No midbrain atrophy or hummingbird sign. No hot cross bun sign. The putaminal changes raise the possibility of multiple system atrophy, parkinsonian type (MSA-P), though findings are subtle. Age-appropriate periventricular white matter changes. No structural cause for parkinsonism identified.
recommended_actions: DaTscan (dopamine transporter SPECT) to confirm presynaptic dopaminergic deficit
Autonomic function testing to evaluate for MSA
Consider cardiac MIBG scintigraphy to differentiate PD from MSA
Follow-up MRI in 12 months to assess for interval progression of putaminal changes

labs.interpretation: Complete laboratory evaluation is unremarkable. CBC and BMP are within normal limits. Thyroid function is normal, ruling out hypothyroid-related psychomotor slowing. Ceruloplasmin is normal, excluding Wilson disease. Vitamin B12 is normal. RPR is non-reactive. Inflammatory markers are normal. Liver function tests and metabolic markers are unremarkable. No laboratory abnormalities to suggest a secondary or reversible cause of parkinsonism.

signal_characteristics.right_putamen: Severely reduced uptake, nearly absent in posterior putamen
signal_characteristics.left_putamen: Severely reduced uptake, nearly absent in posterior putamen
signal_characteristics.striatal_binding_ratio: Right caudate 1.8 (normal >2.5), Left caudate 1.7 (normal >2.5), Right putamen 0.6 (normal >2.5), Left putamen 0.7 (normal >2.5)

additional_observations: Symmetric and severely reduced bilateral putaminal dopamine transporter uptake
Caudate nuclei also show moderate reduction bilaterally
Pattern demonstrates diffuse, symmetric presynaptic dopaminergic denervation
The symmetric pattern with severe putaminal loss and moderate caudate involvement is more suggestive of an atypical parkinsonian syndrome than idiopathic PD
output.impression: Abnormal DaTscan demonstrating severely reduced dopamine transporter uptake bilaterally, symmetric, with near-complete loss of posterior putaminal binding and moderate reduction in caudate binding. This confirms presynaptic dopaminergic deficit. The symmetric and diffuse pattern is atypical for idiopathic Parkinson's disease, which typically shows asymmetric posterior putaminal loss. This pattern raises concern for an atypical parkinsonian disorder such as MSA-P.
recommended_actions: Levodopa challenge test to assess dopaminergic responsiveness
Cardiac MIBG scintigraphy to differentiate PD from MSA
Clinical follow-up in 6 months to reassess progression rate

Levodopa_Challenge_Test.clinical_significance: Moderate parkinsonian motor impairment at baseline
Only 16% improvement, below the 30% threshold for clinically meaningful response
Subthreshold levodopa response; poor response argues against idiopathic PD and favors atypical parkinsonism

output.interpretation: Levodopa challenge test shows a poor motor response, with only 15.6% improvement in UPDRS-III motor score (below the 30% threshold considered supportive of idiopathic Parkinson's disease). While a single acute challenge does not definitively exclude PD, the poor response in the context of symmetric parkinsonism, early postural instability, and prominent autonomic dysfunction is more consistent with an atypical parkinsonian syndrome, particularly MSA-P. A chronic levodopa trial over several weeks may be considered but expectations should be tempered.

Tilt_Table_Test.clinical_significance: Neurogenic orthostatic hypotension: systolic drop of 40 mmHg and diastolic drop of 24 mmHg
Inadequate compensatory tachycardia, consistent with cardiovagal impairment

Cardiovagal_Function.test: Heart rate variability to deep breathing

Thermoregulatory_Sweat_Test.clinical_significance: Extensive preganglionic sudomotor failure, involving trunk and proximal limbs

output.interpretation: Comprehensive autonomic testing reveals widespread and severe autonomic failure. There is definite neurogenic orthostatic hypotension with a systolic drop exceeding 30 mmHg and inadequate heart rate compensation. QSART demonstrates a combined preganglionic and postganglionic sudomotor pattern with length-dependent loss. Cardiovagal function is impaired. Thermoregulatory sweat test reveals 42% anterior body anhidrosis, which is extensive. The combination of severe, widespread autonomic failure occurring early in the disease course (within 2 years of motor onset and some autonomic symptoms predating motor symptoms) is highly suggestive of multiple system atrophy rather than idiopathic Parkinson's disease, in which autonomic dysfunction is typically milder and develops later.
abnormal_values_summary: Neurogenic orthostatic hypotension: 40 mmHg systolic drop with inadequate HR compensation
QSART: Severely reduced distally in legs and feet
Heart rate variability to deep breathing: 4.2 (reduced)

Neuropsychological_Battery.clinical_significance: Borderline, with deficits in executive function and delayed recall
Impaired processing speed, consistent with subcortical dysfunction
Mildly impaired delayed recall, though retrieval benefit with cueing
Impaired set-shifting, consistent with frontal-subcortical dysfunction
Reduced phonemic fluency, consistent with frontal-subcortical pattern

output.interpretation: Neuropsychological profile reveals a mild frontal-subcortical pattern of cognitive impairment with reduced processing speed, impaired set-shifting, and reduced verbal fluency. Visuospatial function and language are preserved. Memory shows mild encoding-retrieval dissociation with benefit from cueing, consistent with subcortical rather than cortical (hippocampal) memory dysfunction. This pattern is nonspecific and can be seen in both idiopathic Parkinson's disease and atypical parkinsonian syndromes including MSA. The mild severity is consistent with early disease.
abnormal_values_summary: CVLT-3 Long Delay Free Recall: z = -1.2 (mildly impaired)

Polysomnography.clinical_significance: Markedly elevated REM without atonia, confirming REM sleep behavior disorder
Video-documented dream enactment with vocalizations and upper limb movements
Mildly elevated PLMS, often associated with synucleinopathies
Absence of stridor; stridor would strongly suggest MSA

output.interpretation: Polysomnography confirms REM sleep behavior disorder with markedly elevated REM without atonia (tonic 58%, phasic 44%) and three video-documented dream-enactment episodes. No obstructive sleep apnea. Mildly elevated periodic limb movement index. Notably, no nocturnal stridor was detected, which, if present, would strongly support MSA. REM sleep behavior disorder is a well-established prodromal marker of synucleinopathies including Parkinson's disease, multiple system atrophy, and dementia with Lewy bodies. Its presence in this patient preceded motor symptoms, which is characteristic of alpha-synuclein disorders.
abnormal_values_summary: REM without atonia: Tonic 58%, Phasic 44% (markedly elevated, confirms RBD)
Reduced total sleep time and efficiency

additional_observations: Normal cardiac MIBG uptake, with preserved heart-to-mediastinum ratios on both early and delayed images

In the context of parkinsonism with autonomic failure, preserved cardiac MIBG uptake is more consistent with MSA than with idiopathic Parkinson's disease, in which postganglionic sympathetic cardiac denervation typically results in reduced MIBG uptake
output.impression: Normal cardiac MIBG scintigraphy with preserved heart-to-mediastinum ratios. In the setting of parkinsonism with significant autonomic failure, this finding argues against idiopathic Parkinson's disease (which typically shows reduced cardiac MIBG uptake reflecting postganglionic cardiac sympathetic denervation) and supports a diagnosis of MSA, in which the autonomic failure is preganglionic and cardiac MIBG uptake is typically preserved.
recommended_actions: Correlate with clinical progression rate and levodopa responsiveness
Consider genetic testing if family history is suggestive
Multidisciplinary evaluation for symptomatic management of autonomic failure

output.query: multiple system atrophy diagnostic criteria differentiation from Parkinson disease

results.title: Second consensus statement on the diagnosis of multiple system atrophy
results.authors: Gilman S, Wenning GK, Low PA, et al.
results.key_finding: MSA diagnostic criteria: probable MSA requires autonomic failure (orthostatic hypotension with ≥30 mmHg systolic drop or urinary incontinence) plus poorly levodopa-responsive parkinsonism or cerebellar ataxia

results.title: MDS criteria for the diagnosis of multiple system atrophy
results.authors: Wenning GK, Stankovic I, Vignatelli L, et al.
results.key_finding: Updated MDS MSA criteria introduce clinically established and clinically probable categories. Clinically established MSA requires: sporadic, progressive disease with onset ≥30 years, autonomic dysfunction, parkinsonism or cerebellar ataxia, and at least one supportive feature (e.g., early postural instability, rapid progression, poor levodopa response)

results.title: Cardiac MIBG scintigraphy in differentiating MSA from PD
results.authors: Nagayama H, Hamamoto M, Ueda M, et al.
results.key_finding: Cardiac MIBG uptake is typically preserved in MSA (preganglionic autonomic failure) and reduced in PD (postganglionic sympathetic denervation), providing useful diagnostic differentiation

output.summary: Current diagnostic criteria for MSA require autonomic failure plus poorly levodopa-responsive parkinsonism or cerebellar ataxia. Key differentiators from PD include: symmetric parkinsonism, early postural instability, poor levodopa response, severe early autonomic failure, preserved cardiac MIBG uptake (vs. reduced in PD), and specific MRI findings such as putaminal atrophy with a hyperintense rim or hot cross bun sign. REM sleep behavior disorder occurs in both PD and MSA and is not a differentiating feature.

differential.key_features: Symmetric parkinsonism, early postural instability, severe early autonomic failure (orthostatic hypotension, erectile dysfunction, constipation), poor levodopa response, preserved cardiac MIBG, putaminal changes on MRI
RBD is highly associated with PD; bradykinesia and rigidity are present; however symmetric onset, poor levodopa response, early severe autonomic failure, and preserved MIBG argue against
Early postural instability and falls could suggest PSP, but no vertical gaze palsy, no midbrain atrophy on MRI, and autonomic failure pattern is more consistent with MSA
RBD and parkinsonism could suggest DLB, but no visual hallucinations, no fluctuating cognition, and autonomic failure severity is more consistent with MSA
Lower body predominance could overlap, but MRI shows no significant vascular burden, no stepwise progression, and presence of RBD and autonomic failure points to synucleinopathy

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed history focusing on symmetry of motor onset, timeline of autonomic symptoms relative to motor symptoms, and functional decline trajectory
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Symmetric motor onset with autonomic symptoms (constipation, ED, RBD) predating motor symptoms by 1-2 years; relatively rapid progression over 2 years

optimal_actions.action: Perform comprehensive neurological examination with emphasis on symmetry of findings, postural stability, orthostatic vitals, and cerebellar signs
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Symmetric rigidity and bradykinesia, early postural instability, neurogenic orthostatic hypotension (>30 mmHg systolic drop), no cerebellar signs

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with attention to putaminal, pontine, and cerebellar morphology
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Subtle putaminal atrophy with hyperintense rim; no cerebellar or pontine atrophy; no midbrain atrophy

optimal_actions.action: Order basic laboratory workup including CBC, BMP, thyroid function, ceruloplasmin, vitamin B12, RPR
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal results, ruling out secondary causes of parkinsonism

optimal_actions.action: Order DaTscan to confirm presynaptic dopaminergic deficit
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Symmetric, severely reduced putaminal DAT uptake (atypical pattern for PD)
Poor levodopa response (<30% improvement in UPDRS-III), supporting atypical parkinsonism

optimal_actions.action: Order comprehensive autonomic testing (tilt table, QSART, thermoregulatory sweat test)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Severe widespread autonomic failure with neurogenic orthostatic hypotension and sudomotor dysfunction

optimal_actions.action: Consider cardiac MIBG scintigraphy to differentiate PD from MSA
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Preserved cardiac MIBG uptake, supporting MSA over PD

optimal_actions.action: Order polysomnography to formally document REM sleep behavior disorder
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Confirmed RBD with elevated REM without atonia; no stridor

optimal_actions.action: Initiate multidisciplinary management: midodrine or droxidopa for orthostatic hypotension, physical therapy for balance and fall prevention, speech therapy for hypophonia

critical_actions: Recognize that symmetric parkinsonism with early severe autonomic failure is a red flag for atypical parkinsonism, not idiopathic PD
Perform orthostatic vital signs to document neurogenic orthostatic hypotension
Assess levodopa responsiveness before committing to a PD diagnosis
Order autonomic function testing to characterize the extent and pattern of autonomic failure
Consider MSA in the differential diagnosis and communicate diagnostic uncertainty to the patient
Initiate treatment for orthostatic hypotension to prevent falls and syncope
contraindicated_actions: Diagnosing idiopathic Parkinson's disease without assessing levodopa responsiveness and autonomic function
Prescribing high-dose levodopa without recognizing the poor response pattern
Prescribing anticholinergics (e.g., trihexyphenidyl) which would worsen autonomic dysfunction and orthostatic hypotension
Attributing orthostatic hypotension solely to antihypertensive medication without performing autonomic testing
Prescribing dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole) which are more likely to exacerbate orthostatic hypotension in MSA than in PD
key_reasoning_points: Symmetric parkinsonism from onset (without clear lateralization) is atypical for idiopathic PD, which characteristically begins asymmetrically
Absence of resting tremor, while possible in PD (akinetic-rigid variant), combined with other atypical features increases concern for MSA

Autonomic symptoms (constipation, erectile dysfunction, RBD) predating motor symptoms by 1-2 years, followed by early postural instability, suggest a synucleinopathy but with a pattern more consistent with MSA than PD
Neurogenic orthostatic hypotension with >30 mmHg systolic drop and inadequate heart rate compensation within 2 years of motor symptom onset is a red flag for MSA
Poor levodopa response (<30% UPDRS-III improvement) strongly argues against idiopathic PD
Preserved cardiac MIBG uptake differentiates MSA (preganglionic autonomic failure) from PD (postganglionic sympathetic denervation)
MRI showing subtle putaminal changes (hyperintense rim, possible iron deposition) is consistent with MSA-P
The combination of clinical features meets MDS criteria for clinically probable MSA: sporadic progressive adult-onset disease with autonomic failure and poorly levodopa-responsive parkinsonism

metadata.author: NeuroBench case generation system
metadata.diagnostic_challenge: Differentiating MSA-P from idiopathic Parkinson's disease in a patient presenting with symmetric parkinsonism, early autonomic failure, and poor levodopa response
metadata.educational_value: Teaches recognition of red flags for atypical parkinsonism and the systematic approach to differentiating synucleinopathies
metadata.key_teaching_point: Early severe autonomic failure, symmetric parkinsonism without tremor, and poor levodopa response are hallmarks of MSA-P that distinguish it from idiopathic PD
